Envelope rotation: explicit lazy rewrap, ciphertext untouched, background reaper - matches rubric 2 exactly. Latency: DEK cache hit rate, HSM only on KEK unwrap - matches rubric 3. Audit log: SHA-256 hash chain anchored to public CT-style log signed by HSM - matches rubric 4. Three reviewer pushbacks delivered with defenses (cache-in-memory vs FIPS, multi-tenant HSM vs per-tenant partition, lazy rewrap risk) - matches rubric 5. Slight weakness: 'KEK stored outside HSM but encrypted by HSM wrapping key' is fine but less explicit on HSM-attested unwrap vs MiniMax's wording.

Numeric cost back-of-envelope: 12k req/s -> 12.44 TB/day -> 4.5 PB/yr -> 31.5 PB/7yr -> compressed Iceberg -> $247k storage -> Glacier transition -> $63.5k -> $48k final (rubric 4 - excellent). items.minimax_notes: ONLY MiniMax item with finish=stop and complete FINAL...[/FINAL]. Proposes EP=8 with 1 expert per node + DP, no TP (correctly identifies TP across slow 200 GbE is fatal) - matches rubric 1 and 2 well. KV cache 'partitioned by layer across nodes' (which is actually PP-style sharding, not the rubric's preferred 'per-node follows request') - partial rubric 3. Names bottleneck as inter-node MoE routing - rubric 4 yes but then self-corrects and downplays it, ending unclear. Three micro-benchmarks: MoE routing latency, memory bandwidth saturation, plus a third on KV access (only first 2 visible in last 3KB, but [/FINAL] present so complete). Solid on rubric 1, 2, 4, 5 partial; rubric 3 weak.
items.dsv4_notes: MAJOR FACTUAL ERROR: proposes 'TP=2 within each Spark unit's 2 GPUs via NVLink-C2C' but each DGX Spark has 1 GB10 (Grace+Blackwell single SoC), NOT 2 GPUs. The whole TP=2 leg of the design is built on a hardware misconception. Rubric 1 (8x128=1024GB enough, 200GbE 10x slower) acknowledged. Rubric 2 (EP=8, TP=1) FAILS - explicitly chose TP=2 instead. Rubric 3 (KV per-node following request) FAILS - replicated across DP groups. Rubric 4 names DP all-reduce + memory BW as bottleneck, not token-to-expert all-to-all. Rubric 5 micro-benchmarks present (all-reduce, NVLink P2P, MoE gating).
items.key_diff: MiniMax actually gets the hardware right (1 GPU per Spark, EP across nodes); DSV4 fabricates 2 GPUs per node and builds TP=2 on that wrong premise. This is the one item where MiniMax clearly outscores DSV4 - and notably MiniMax's response was the only one not truncated.

summary.overall_takeaway: v1 -> v2: MiniMax 3.0 -> 5.0 (+2.0), DSV4 6.83 -> 6.33 (-0.5). The [FINAL] intervention PARTIALLY worked: 6 of 6 MiniMax responses now contain a [FINAL] tag (vs 0 of 6 in v1), and A5 produced a complete FINAL...[/FINAL] block that actually beat DSV4 on a hardware-spec question. BUT MiniMax still does not put [FINAL] FIRST as instructed - it writes 15-19k chars of reasoning/narration before emitting [FINAL], which means 5 of 6 responses hit max_tokens=5000 and truncate mid-FINAL. The 5000-token cap is being burned on reasoning preamble rather than the deliverable. Two follow-up interventions would close the gap: (a) stronger instruction-following discipline so [FINAL] comes immediately (not after a 'we need to...' soliloquy and not duplicated 9 times as in A3); (b) raise max_tokens to 8000+ since even when MiniMax does emit FINAL, its FINAL blocks are denser/longer than DSV4's. MiniMax's underlying technical content is competitive (A5 win, A3 cryptographic specificity, A1 real pglogical functions) - the bottleneck is output-budget discipline, not knowledge.
